selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I don't do social media myself, and honestly, that's probably been one of the better decisions I've made in the last few years. But I see it every single day working at the coffee shop here in LA. People come in staring at their phones, sharing things they haven't even verified, getting upset about stories that might not even be real. As someone raising four kids on a part-time barista salary, I've had to be pretty practical about what information I trust and what I don't. The way I see it, dealing with fake content boils down to a few straightforward things that don't require you to be some tech expert. First, you've got to slow down. I know that sounds simple, but people share things instantly without thinking. When you see something that upsets you or seems too crazy to be true, it probably is. Take five minutes before you pass it along to your friends or family. Check if it's coming from a source you actually recognize and trust. With my kids, I've taught them to ask basic questions: where did this come from? Who benefits from people believing this? That's not complicated stuff, just common sense. Second, diversify where you get information. Don't rely on just one source or one platform. Read different perspectives, talk to people face to face when you can. That's something I value because I'm not naturally outgoing, but having real conversations about what's happening in the world beats arguing with strangers online any day. Third, understand that fake content often plays on emotions, especially fear and anger. When I'm feeling down, which happens more than I'd like, I notice I'm more vulnerable to believing negative stories. So I try to check my emotional state before I engage with heavy news. And honestly, if social media is making you feel worse about life, maybe the answer is just to step back from it like I did. You don't need it to live a full life. You need real connections, real information you can verify, and the discipline to question what you see. That's how you protect yourself.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: This persona is a 43-year-old widowed barista with four children, moderate conscientiousness, low openness, and moderate-to-high neuroticism, who explicitly avoids social media. His perspective should reflect pragmatism born from limited resources, skepticism due to his psychological state, and reliance on practical, ground-level observations from his work environment. His low extraversion and agreeableness suggest he won't be overly diplomatic but rather direct. His hobbies (running, painting, gardening) suggest someone who finds grounding in tangible, real-world activities. The absence of social media usage means he approaches the topic as an observer and concerned parent rather than an active user, giving him unique credibility in advocating for skepticism and distance from platforms. His moderate conscientiousness means he can follow systematic approaches but might not be overly detailed. His depression means he understands emotional vulnerability to misinformation.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, slightly weary but caring, grounded in real-world observation rather than theory, protective (especially regarding his children), skeptical of digital solutions, honest about personal struggles, non-judgmental toward others caught in misinformation but firm about the importance of verification
used_persona_fields: ocean scores (conscientiousness, openness, extraversion, agreeableness, neuroticism)
